title: Ban on inverters from high-risk countries, led by China, to affect 14% of EU solar demand through 2030
gist: Wood Mackenzie estimates the EU ban on Chinese and other high-risk-country PV inverters from EU-funded clean energy projects will affect about 14% of European solar demand (over 28 GWdc) and 12% of storage deployments through 2030, hitting Central and Eastern Europe hardest, though ~80% of demand in privately/nationally funded channels keeps Chinese dominance intact for now.

insights.claim: The European Commission's ban on PV inverters and power-conversion systems from China and other high-risk countries in EU-funded clean energy projects may affect around 14% of forecast European solar PV demand from 2026 to 2030, totaling over 28 GWdc of inverter demand.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: Quantifies a redirected 28 GWdc of inverter demand away from Chinese vendors, a tailwind for European inverter makers.
insights.quote: may affect around 14% of forecasted European solar PV demand from 2026 to 2030, totalling over 28 GWdc of PV inverter demand

insights.claim: The ban is also expected to impact about 12% of forecast EU energy storage deployments over 2026-2030, with utility-scale storage facing the greatest exposure.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: Utility-scale storage procurement faces the most disruption, forcing unbundling of integrated battery-inverter systems.
insights.quote: 12% of forecast energy storage deployments will also be impacted over the same period, with utility-scale storage facing the greatest exposure
insights.locator: Potentially, 12% of forecast energy storage deployments

insights.claim: Chinese suppliers accounted for over 80% of inverter shipments to Europe in 2025, which the ban is explicitly designed to address.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: Baseline Chinese market share of 80%+ frames how much share is contestable if restrictions widen.
insights.quote: Chinese suppliers accounted for over 80% of inverter shipments to Europe in 2025
insights.locator: The ban, which seeks to address Chinese vendor domination

insights.claim: Central and Eastern European countries including Romania, Bulgaria, Czechia, the Baltic states, and Greece will be most affected because EU funding is more prevalent there.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: Country-level exposure concentrates disruption in price-sensitive CEE markets where premiums bite hardest.
insights.quote: Romania, Bulgaria, Czechia, the Baltic states and Greece are among the most exposed markets
insights.locator: The ban, which seeks to address Chinese vendor domination

insights.claim: Although European-made inverter cost premiums are substantial, the overall impact on total project costs is modest at 2% to 8% depending on market segment.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: Manageable 2-8% project cost impact suggests the ban is more of a supply-chain reshuffle than a demand killer.
insights.quote: the overall impact on total project costs is more modest, in the range of 2% to 8% depending on market segment
insights.locator: While inverter cost premiums for European-made alternatives

insights.claim: Procurement complications, design changes, and forced unbundling of integrated battery-inverter systems present bigger challenges than cost, particularly in price-sensitive Eastern European markets.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: Non-cost friction (unbundling, redesign) is the real drag, hurting vendors of integrated battery-inverter products.
insights.quote: the forced unbundling of integrated battery-inverter systems present additional challenges, particularly in price-sensitive Eastern European markets
insights.locator: However, cost is not the only disruptive factor

insights.claim: The ban extends beyond EU borders to utility-scale solar projects in North Africa, the Middle East, and the Caspian region where they receive EU institutional financing.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: EU-financed projects abroad also exclude Chinese inverters, extending the addressable share shift beyond Europe.
insights.quote: with utility-scale solar projects in North Africa, the Middle East and the Caspian region subject to the restriction where they receive EU institutional financing
insights.locator: The effects of the ban will also extend beyond EU borders

insights.claim: Pending revisions to the EU Cybersecurity Act could expand the ban to cover all solar PV inverters and storage power conversion systems regardless of funding source, dramatically expanding the scope of restrictions.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: The key upside catalyst for European vendors is Cybersecurity Act reform treating inverters as critical infrastructure.
insights.quote: pending revisions to the EU Cybersecurity Act could expand the ban to cover all solar PV inverters and storage Power Conversion Systems (PCS), regardless of the source of funding
insights.locator: Also, pending revisions to the EU Cybersecurity Act

insights.claim: The ban represents roughly 4 to 5 GW per year of demand moving away from Chinese vendors through 2030, but roughly 80% of European solar and storage demand still flows through private and nationally funded channels where Chinese dominance remains intact.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: The near-term impact is bounded; the trade only becomes structural if member states extend restrictions to national funding.
insights.quote: roughly 80% of European solar and storage demand flows through private and nationally funded channels, where Chinese inverter dominance will remain intact for now
insights.locator: This ban represents a meaningful shift

insights.claim: If EU Member States adopt the restriction for solar and storage projects funded from their own national budgets, the share of capacity affected would expand significantly beyond current estimates.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: Member-state adoption is the swing factor that could multiply the disruption; watch national policy responses.
insights.quote: If Member States comply, the share of capacity affected would expand significantly beyond the current estimates.
insights.locator: Critically, the European Commission is also asking
